method_atom: a systematic perturbative expansion of an interacting many-body system in a small dimensionless coupling parameter, organized by an effective field theory that separates a dominant low-energy interaction scale from higher-order, non-universal corrections, together with a family of self-consistent resummation and approximation schemes (mean-field/Bogoliubov-type, self-consistent Hartree-Fock, Phi-derivable/two-particle-irreducible, optimized/variational perturbation theory, 1/N expansion, and renormalization-group techniques) used to compute ground-state and equilibrium properties and to locate a symmetry-breaking transition as a control parameter is varied.
object_atom: the three-dimensional dilute homogeneous gas of weakly interacting bosonic atoms, characterized by an s-wave scattering length and number density, at zero and finite temperature — including its ground-state energy, condensate depletion, long-wavelength collective excitations, and the critical temperature of Bose-Einstein condensation.
brief_reason: The small-parameter perturbative expansion plus self-consistent resummation machinery is the transferable method, while the dilute gas of interacting bosons with a scattering length undergoing condensation is the domain-specific object.
